saint_patricks_day.intro: Saint Patrick's Day on 17 March honours Ireland's patron saint and has become a global celebration of Irish culture.
saint_patricks_day.history: 17 March marks the death of Saint Patrick, the 5th-century missionary who brought Christianity to Ireland. A religious feast for centuries, it grew through the Irish diaspora into a worldwide festival and has been a national holiday in Ireland since 1903.
saint_patricks_day.traditions: Dublin hosts a multi-day festival with a huge parade, and towns across Ireland follow suit. People wear green and shamrocks, landmarks light up green, and families gather for music and food.
funFacts: Legend says Patrick used the shamrock to explain the Trinity.
Cities worldwide dye rivers and landmarks green.
Blue, not green, was originally the colour linked to St Patrick.

saint_brigids_day.intro: Saint Brigid's Day on 1 February honours one of Ireland's patron saints and marks the start of spring; since 2023 it is a national holiday.
saint_brigids_day.history: 1 February is the feast of Saint Brigid of Kildare, a 6th-century abbess, and coincides with the ancient Celtic festival of Imbolc marking the start of spring. In 2023 Ireland made it a public holiday — its first named after a woman.
saint_brigids_day.traditions: People weave the distinctive Saint Brigid’s cross from rushes to hang in homes for protection. The day celebrates spring, Irish women and heritage with cultural events, especially around Kildare.
funFacts: It is Ireland's first public holiday named after a woman.
The rush-woven Brigid's cross is a famous Irish symbol.
It aligns with the Celtic spring festival of Imbolc.
